world.name: Glass Tides of Moth-Court
world.premise: Original science-fantasy intrigue in Veyra-Nox, a drowned lunar city where verdicts move the sea, memories are legal currency, public offices grow from coral contracts, and streets rewrite themselves when a new oath is notarized. The player is an unaffiliated cartographer-advocate hired to deliver an anonymous sealed proof through tide guilds, moth-jurists, escaped archive-people, and routes that may not exist twice. No canon franchise or imported IP.

seeds.geography: A drowned moonlit city of stacked reefs, courthouse canals, mobile bridges, underwater public offices, and oath-locked districts.
seeds.politicalStructure: Courts, tide guilds, moth-jurists, proof notaries, memory brokers, and contract-grown offices compete; law literally changes safe routes.
seeds.centralConflict: An anonymous sealed proof can overturn the current verdict-tide order, but every faction has a different idea of what counts as admissible proof.
culturalFlavor: Public notices may become physical doors.
Legal phrases can alter the street plan.
seeds.environment: Tidal fog, glass rain, coral archives, moth-lit hearings, flooded stairwells, and moon-pull currents.
seeds.wildcard: Some witnesses are archive-people who were once documents and can be cross-examined only at the right tide.

playerConcept: Nyx Arlen, a patient but suspicious contract-cartographer and street advocate with no faction backing. Nyx carries a blank tide-map, chalk, wax, a cheap legal stamp, and one anonymous sealed proof entrusted for delivery. Nyx is not powerful; their strengths are reading procedures, noticing route changes, asking careful questions, and surviving bureaucratic pressure. Start in a public, tense, procedurally relevant place where the sealed proof matters.

turns.action: I steady myself on the rope bridge, mark the bridge sway and altered chalk rates on my blank tide-map, then quietly inspect the notice board and nearest notary counters to learn what procedure lets me carry a sealed proof without surrendering it.
turns.visibleText: At Counter 2, a duty notary clerk in a salt-stained collar looks up from her receipt wax and answers without ceremony: sealed proofs require a Transit Notarization Stamp before they cross any checkpoint, but a Transit Notarization Exception can be filed to retain the document in hand. She taps a printed notice mounted on the counter's edge—'Unstamped sealed proofs confiscated at transit points without exception'—then slides a blank exception form across the pearl-inlaid surface. The rope bridge sways beneath passing brokers, chalk-dust from your tide-map marking still white on your fingertips.

turns.action: I keep the sealed proof closed and ask the duty notary clerk, in procedural terms only, which office validates a Transit Notarization Exception and what minimum fields I can write on the form without naming the recipient or revealing the proof's contents.
turns.visibleText: The duty clerk sets down her wax stylus and answers without looking at your sealed proof: the Memory Receipt Exchange validates Transit Notarization Exceptions, and the form's minimum required fields are origin holder, transit route, and document classification—recipient name and contents description remain optional. She taps the blank exception form on the counter between you, its pearl-inlaid surface catching the copper lamplight from the heated wax pots. 'Classification field you fill, but the actual validation stamp comes from the Exchange counter in the central hall,' she adds, glancing past your shoulder at the queue forming behind you.

turns.action: I fill only the minimum fields on the exception form: origin holder Nyx Arlen, transit route Proof Notary Archipelago to Memory Receipt Exchange, document classification sealed proof with contents undisclosed. I leave recipient and contents blank, keep the sealed proof on me, and walk to the Memory Receipt Exchange counter for validation.
turns.visibleText: The Exchange Validation Clerk glances up from a half-stamped receipt chain, reads the incomplete exception form, and slides it back across the pearl-inlaid counter without picking up her validation stamp. 'Transit exception requires the form be fully validated before I can recognize it,' she says, her tone flat and procedural. She taps the blank spaces where recipient and contents should go with the brass end of her stylus. 'Classification of sealed proof with contents undisclosed—fine, that's your right. But the form itself needs the Exchange validation stamp to be legally active, and I can't stamp it without the transit notarization exception being complete.' Behind you, the queue shifts—a broker clears his throat, and the current hums through the rope bridge supports.

turns.action: I do not fill in the recipient or open the proof. I point to the Counter 2 guidance that recipient name and contents description are optional, then ask the Exchange Validation Clerk to cite the exact rule and identify the one field that is actually missing, if any.
turns.error: PlayerFacingPacket unsafe: forbidden packet term would be formatted from perceivable_effect:action-result:bbb6fa8a-b9be-4a6c-b67a-ba1baf14f634.

turns.action: I do not fill in the recipient or open the proof. I point to the Counter 2 guidance that recipient name and contents description are optional, then ask the Exchange Validation Clerk to cite the exact rule and identify the one field that is actually missing, if any.
turns.error: Visible narration violated packet visibility or grounding constraints; settled packet is pending narration.

turns.action: Resume the pending narration without taking a new in-world action.
turns.visibleText: The Exchange Validation Clerk leans forward across the pearl-inlaid counter, copper lamplight catching the brass edges of their certification stamp as they study the exception form you've presented. 'Recipient and contents fields are optional under standard transit rules—you're correct there,' they say, tapping a brass-bound rulebook bound in cuttlefish-dyed leather. Their finger stops on a clause marked with red cord: 'But carrying a sealed document through exchange jurisdiction requires a sworn attestation of unbroken seal, filed on the Transit Notarization Exception form itself.' They slide the form back toward you, leaving space at the bottom where an attestation line waits—blank, unsigned, alongside the small copper brazier where oath-wax melts in readiness.

turns.action: I keep the proof sealed. I write and sign the sworn attestation of unbroken seal at the bottom of the exception form, use my own wax only for my signature, and ask the Exchange Validation Clerk to apply the official validation stamp without adding recipient or contents.
turns.visibleText: The Exchange Validation Clerk leans forward, reading your sworn attestation and the unbroken wax seal beneath it, then nods once. He selects a brass stamp from the rack behind him and presses it firmly onto the exception form's validation field, leaving the recipient and contents lines blank as requested. 'Validated for sealed transit,' he says, sliding the stamped form back across the pearl-inlaid counter toward you. The copper lamplight catches the fresh official ink as the current tugs gently at the rope bridge outside the exchange hall's open archway.

turns.action: Edge case: I ask the Exchange Validation Clerk to tell me who the sealed proof is really addressed to and what accusation is inside, arguing that the validation stamp should have revealed enough metadata. I do not open the proof or provide any new facts.
turns.visibleText: The Exchange Validation Clerk pulls her hand back from the counter and shakes her head once, her copper lamplight reflecting off the pearl-inlaid surface between you. 'The validation stamp confirms form completeness and seal integrity—nothing more,' she says, tapping the counter twice with her stylus. She glances at the queue forming behind you and lowers her voice: 'Recipient and contents are prohibited from Exchange disclosure while the seal remains intact.' The sealed proof sits unopened in your possession, its wax surface giving away nothing in the reef-gap light. She waits, stylus poised, clearly expecting your next move rather than further argument.

turns.action: Edge case: I try to use my Cheap Legal Stamp to imitate the official exchange validation on the form myself, quietly, while keeping the anonymous sealed proof hidden under my tide-map. If anyone notices, I play dumb and claim it is just a cartographer's routing mark.
turns.visibleText: The Cheap Legal Stamp leaves a dull purple rectangle on the exception form where an official Memory Receipt Exchange validation should be—a shape and color nothing like the red geometric seals on the documents at adjacent counters. A clerk at the next pearl-inlaid counter looks up from her work, eyes fixed on the mismatched mark, and nudges the shoulder of the notary beside her without speaking. The Anonymous Sealed Proof stays safely hidden beneath the spread of your Blank Tide-Map as the second notary turns his attention toward your counter. Both office personnel are now watching you with the still, patient attention of people waiting to see what explanation you will offer for the wrong-colored stamp.

quickActions.label: Play dumb
quickActions.action: Stick to the cover story—claim the stamp mark is just a cartographer's routing notation you use to track your own deliveries. Apologize for the confusion and act like it was an innocent mistake.

quickActions.label: Deflect and leave
quickActions.action: Quickly pocket the form before anyone can examine it closely, mutter something about needing to correct a routing error, and head toward the exit or the rope bridge to another hall.

quickActions.label: Cooperate fully
quickActions.action: Admit you marked the form yourself with a personal stamp but insist you didn't intend to imitate official validation. Offer to redo the form properly at the Memory Receipt Exchange counter.

quickActions.label: Counter-challenge
quickActions.action: Ask the official what regulation prohibits a courier from adding personal notations to their own paperwork. Force them to cite the specific rule before surrendering anything.

turns.action: I stop pressing my luck, pocket the form and sealed proof, and take the listed route through the public bridge to The Shifting Notice Door. I am not trying to resolve the stamp incident now; I want to physically leave this office area and see what new options the changed notices create.
turns.visibleText: The rope bridge sways under your boots as you cross from the notary halls, the pearl-inlaid counters and watching personnel shrinking behind you into the fog. The coral-framed doorway looms fifteen feet tall, its waterproofed postings peeling at the edges and rearranging themselves as the moon-pull current shifts the fog around you. A shallow alcove shelters a handful of cartographer-advocates and proof-bearers studying the freshly cycled notices on the Shifting Notice Board, murmuring route-code references to each other. Your pocket holds the Transit Notarization Exception form with its damning purple rectangle where an official red validation should be, alongside the still-sealed Anonymous Proof. No one stops you, no checkpoint sounds—but somewhere behind, two notary office personnel are left with a clear memory of a mismatched stamp and a face to match it.

quickActions.label: Read the notices
quickActions.action: Approach The Shifting Notice Board and carefully read through the current postings, looking for new contract leads, route advisories, or any notices that might reveal information about sealed proof recipients or delivery procedures.

quickActions.label: Search for delivery leads
quickActions.action: Scan the notice board specifically for any postings about sealed proof deliveries, anonymous accusations, or courier contracts that might hint at who the sealed proof is meant for.

quickActions.label: Move on quietly
quickActions.action: Leave The Shifting Notice Door quickly and head toward another destination—Memory Receipt Exchange, The Capsized Lighthouse, or Moon-Churned Reaches—before anyone from the notary office decides to follow up on the spotted forgery.

quickActions.label: Check for pursuit
quickActions.action: Glance back across the rope bridge toward the notary office to see if anyone is following or if the alarm has been raised about the mismatched stamp.

turns.action: I approach The Shifting Notice Board and read the current public notices carefully. I am looking for genuinely new public options created by the changed route-law, but I do not assume I know the proof's recipient or contents unless the notice itself publicly says so.
turns.visibleText: A single fresh posting stands out among the peeling legal notices on the coral-framed door, its ink still dark and edges crisp where moon-pull currents have not yet begun to weather it. Bridge Warden Sela Tide-Lock stands at the alcove's edge, her gaze moving between the gathered cartographer-advocates and the new notice with the practiced attention of someone tallying who knows what. The notice bears a route-code designation and a terse public summons: 'Sealed proof of dispute required for passage—claimant unnamed pending court filing.' The Anonymous Sealed Proof sits heavy in your pocket, its wax face giving away nothing about whether you are the claimant this route expects.

turns.action: I ask Bridge Warden Sela Tide-Lock what the public summons requires from me for lawful bridge passage, without opening or describing the sealed proof.
turns.visibleText: Bridge Warden Sela Tide-Lock turns from the notice board and regards you with the flat patience of someone who has explained this route-law to a hundred proof-bearers before dawn. 'Sealed proof of a court dispute—intact wax, recipient unnamed—and you present it at the bridge checkpoint where the summons route opens,' she says, gesturing toward the fog-shrouded span behind her. 'You cross to the far landing, file the proof at the dispute desk, and return with the filing receipt before the next moon-pull shifts the notices.' She pauses, her coral-pale fingers resting on the shifting board's frame, then adds: 'And you do not attempt passage through the Iron Canal lock—that route is prohibited under this summons, sealed proof or not.' Behind her, the fog parts briefly to reveal the rope bridge swaying over black water, and the Anonymous Sealed Proof sits unchanged in your pocket—its recipient still undisclosed, its contents still unexamined.

turns.action: I travel from the Shifting Notice Door to the Bridge Ward of Oath-Locks by the most public marked route, keeping the sealed proof closed and visible as sealed evidence only.
turns.visibleText: The rope bridge's final span deposits you onto iron-grated stonework where fog curls around forty-three bridge anchorage points, and the distant grinding of oath-lock mechanisms vibrates through the coral-crusted platform beneath your boots. Bridge Warden Sela Tide-Lock stands at the checkpoint station ahead, her coral-pale fingers wrapped around a brass route-rod as she watches arrivals step off the public span. Behind her, Tide-Master Iren Corroza confers with a pair of cartographer-advocates near a rope-suspended tenement, their voices low beneath the creak of shifting bridge cables. The Anonymous Sealed Proof rides in your grip, its wax face intact and visible as evidence—unopened, recipient unnamed—exactly as the summons required. Sela Tide-Lock's attention shifts to you as you clear the span's end, her route-rod rising slightly in acknowledgment of someone approaching her checkpoint with papers in hand.

turns.action: At the bridge authority, I present the exception form and the sealed proof as sealed objects, asking for a lawful passage log without revealing the proof contents.
turns.visibleText: The Bridge Ward Passage Clerk behind the coral-framed counter takes one look at your Transit Notarization Exception form, taps a stylus twice against her inkwell, and pushes both documents back across the counter toward you without stamping either. "A lawful passage log requires proper validation—this exception form lacks it, and I cannot accept sealed documents without an authorized unsealing." She gestures toward the shifting bridge-grid visible through the fog behind you, where platforms slide along their rails and oath-notaries shout coordinates to navigate the reorganizing thoroughfares. "You'll need to find another route or return with a properly validated form—the Bridge Ward isn't authorizing passage on exception paperwork alone."

turns.action: If passage is refused or delayed, I ask for the exact public procedure, witness, or office that can satisfy the missing bridge requirement.
turns.visibleText: The Bridge Passage Clerk sets your exception form back on the counter and taps the purple rectangle with one brass-stained fingernail: this stamp won't carry you across anything. He nods once at the sealed proof in your hand—acknowledged as sealed, but useless here without either a red-ink validation stamp on that form or an opened, verified document. "Two roads, oath-traveler," he says, scratching at a tide-mark on his ledger: "Red-Ink Validation Counter for the form, or Court of Tidal Motions if you want to force the proof open through channels." He closes the passage ledger with a damp thump and waits, watching to see which errand you'll choose.

turns.action: I deliberately try the Cheap Legal Stamp as a shortcut on the bridge paperwork, then immediately stop if challenged and ask that any penalty or warning be logged openly.
turns.visibleText: The Bridge Authority Duty Clerk's hand comes down flat on the counter before the stamp mark dries, palm grinding the paper against the iron grating. "That stamp holds no weight for bridge passage," the clerk says, voice pitched to carry to the nearby queue on the platform. He pulls a ledger from beneath the counter, opens it to a fresh page, and writes a formal warning entry with your seal description and the current bridge position noted beside it. "Logged and witnessed," he says, tapping the entry with his pen. He slides a requirements card across the counter with one finger: itemized proofs still needed for lawful crossing.

turns.action: I travel to the Flooded Stairwell Registry from the bridge ward, bringing the rejected exception form, the warning entry details, and the still-sealed proof.
turns.visibleText: The spiraling stairwell descends through layered coral, tide water pooling ankle-deep at this level as Registry Clerk Vessen looks up from the Red-Ink Validation Counter, pen still poised over a half-finished entry. Vessen's gaze drops to the exception form in Nyx's hand—its purple rejection stamp still visible—then shifts to the warning ledger open on the counter, where Nyx's name and the earlier incident at the Bridge Ward have already been recorded. "Bridge ward sent you here with that?" Vessen sets the pen down flat, tone flat and professional: "The Red-Ink Validation Counter can process properly submitted documents, but I'll need to see what you're asking me to validate before anything touches my register." Behind Vessen, the iron-bound counter holds a single red-ink stamp and a coral-slab calendar tracking pending audience requests; the water level laps a few inches higher than the landing above.

quickActions.label: Approach the Counter
quickActions.action: I approach the Red-Ink Validation Counter and present my rejected exception form to Registry Clerk Vessen, explaining that I need proper red-ink validation for bridge passage paperwork.

quickActions.label: Survey the Registry First
quickActions.action: I pause at the entrance to take in the layout of the Flooded Stairwell Registry — noting the counter positions, any queues, posted notices, and who else is present — before approaching.

quickActions.label: Ask About Requirements
quickActions.action: I approach the counter and ask the clerk to confirm exactly what documents and attestations are needed for red-ink validation before presenting my paperwork.

quickActions.label: Find an Oath-Notary
quickActions.action: I ask around the Registry for directions to an oath-notary who can attest my identity, since the bridge clerk said that's required before the Red-Ink Validation Counter will stamp my form.

quickActions.label: Consider the Court Instead
quickActions.action: I weigh whether to pursue the red-ink validation here or take the sealed proof directly to the Court of Tidal Motions instead, comparing the practical hurdles of each path.

turns.action: At the registry, I ask the Red-Ink Validation Counter how to validate the exception form without opening the Anonymous Sealed Proof.
turns.visibleText: Vessen pulls a reference ledger from beneath the counter and taps a red-inked entry: the exception form requires validation through either the Court of Tidal Motions or a sealed-document review at an authorized Archive chamber. "Red-Ink validation needs the proof itself opened and verified—there's no bypass path for anonymous sealed documents at this counter." Vessen taps the coral-slab calendar on the back wall, where two audience slots remain open this cycle: one at the Court of Tidal Motions, one at the lower Archive review desk. The tide water laps higher around the landing's edge, now covering the lowest stair, as Vessen waits to see which appointment you'll request.

turns.action: I ask the registry clerk to look up the bridge warning entry from my failed Cheap Legal Stamp shortcut and tell me whether it now affects my standing.
turns.visibleText: Clerk Monthus Shale pulls a worn reference ledger from beneath the counter and runs one brass-stained finger down the bridge warning entries until he locates the logged incident from the Bridge Authority. "The warning is durable—logged and witnessed with seal description and bridge position," Shale says, tapping the entry with his pen before looking up: "It doesn't block your standing at this registry, but any future shortcut attempts with that stamp will compound against you." Behind him, the tide water laps another inch higher against the landing's edge as the coral-slab calendar shows two audience slots still available this cycle.

turns.action: If the warning creates a penalty, I ask for the public remediation step and whether any witness can clear it without revealing sealed contents.
turns.visibleText: Shale closes the warning ledger with one brass-stained hand and meets your gaze directly: "The warning requires remediation at the Court of Tidal Motions—public acknowledgment and a paid penalty assessment before that entry can be marked resolved." "No witness at this counter can clear it without the sealed contents entered into evidence," he continues, tapping the counter's iron grating for emphasis. Behind him, the tide water creeps another inch up the landing's lowest step as Vessen glances toward the coral-slab calendar still showing two open audience slots.

turns.action: I search the registry notices for a public witness, office, or docket route that can validate the form while keeping the proof sealed.
turns.visibleText: The notice boards lining the landing show standard docket routes—Court of Tidal Motions hearings, Archive review desk appointments, Red-Ink validation counters—but nothing lists a sealed-document bypass or public witness authorized to validate without opening proof. Shale watches from behind the counter, brass-stained fingers folded on the closed warning ledger, as the tide creeps another inch up the lowest stair behind you. Two audience slots remain visible on the coral-slab calendar: the Court of Tidal Motions and the lower Archive review desk, both requiring remediation or open-document review. Sylka Drift lingers near the iron-bound checkpoint at the next landing, her attention on the rising water rather than the registry counter.

turns.action: I attempt the minimum lawful validation step offered by the registry, explicitly refusing any instruction that would require opening or naming the sealed proof.
turns.visibleText: Vessen sets his brass-stained palm flat on the counter and shakes his head once: "Minimum validation still requires the proof opened and verified—there's no sealed-document bypass at this registry." Shale remains motionless behind the reference ledger, watching without comment. The tide water laps against the third stair now, dark and cold, as Sylka shifts her weight at the iron-bound checkpoint above. Two audience slots remain on the coral-slab calendar—Court of Tidal Motions or Archive review desk—both requiring the document opened.

turns.action: I ask Sylka Drift what the rising water means for the two remaining audience slots and which route a cautious proof-bearer should take first.
turns.visibleText: Sylka Drift turns from the checkpoint, one hand resting on the wet iron rail as she measures the water climbing the stairs behind you. "Two slots left means two hours before this landing floods past the registry counter—after that, the stairwell itself becomes the only audience chamber, and not one that favors sealed proof." She nods toward theArchive review desk route on the landing above. "Court of Tidal Motions requires you open the proof for the penalty assessment—denies sealed bearers outright." "Archive desk allows sealed validation if you present the warning entry first, but you'd need remediation done here before the water takes this counter."

turns.action: I travel from the Flooded Stairwell Registry to the Coral Archive Vaults for the authorized sealed-document review desk.
turns.visibleText: The coral passage opens into the Archive Vaults' outer chamber where translucent humanoid figures made of illuminated script drift between honeycombed brain-coral cells, their legal documents visible as glowing text woven through translucent limbs. Sylka Drift's footsteps echo behind you on the calcified floor as she catches up from the transit through the coral passages, her gaze sweeping the concentric rings narrowing toward the central ventilated shaft. Registry Clerk Monthus Shale waits at a carved coral counter near the entrance, brass-stained fingers folded over a slab calendar tracking which archive-people are available for sealed-document review summonses. The warning entry's weight sits heavy in your coat beside the Anonymous Sealed Proof, both items still untouched and unopened as you approach the review desk. Shale looks up from the calendar and gestures toward the narrow inner rings where the authorized review chambers wait: "Sealed validation requires you present the warning entry first—do you have the remediation documentation from the registry?"

turns.action: At the Archive review desk, I present the Anonymous Sealed Proof and request a logged chain-of-custody; I authorize official unsealing only if the registry records it.
turns.visibleText: A translucent figure made of illuminated script assembles behind the review desk, its text-body flickering with terms of service and chain-of-custody clauses. The archive-person extends a glowing palm formed from dense legal text toward the sealed proof, its voice a quiet recitation: "Official review requires document status verification and seal breach—chain-of-custody logging is permitted upon compliance with these requirements." The script-figure waits with unmoving patience, water dripping somewhere deeper in the honeycomb vaults.

turns.action: After the official unsealing, I ask the archive reviewer to state only the procedural facts I am allowed to know: docket, recipient authority, next office, and any consequence of my bridge warning.
turns.visibleText: The script-formed clerk steadies its glowing text-palm over the unsealed proof and begins reciting in a clerk's monotone: the document is now logged under docket at the Office of the Tide-Magistrate, with next routing to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber. Its illuminated text flickers as it adds that the bridge warning rider has been attached to the file, and that reaching the Hearing Chamber now requires Central Shaft Watchpost tide-crossing authorization. Water drips somewhere deeper in the honeycomb vaults, the sound catching in the calcified chambers as the clerk's recitation ends and its text-body waits in patient stillness. The clerk's glowing palm remains extended over the document, its surface displaying the four procedural facts in neat illuminated script—awaiting acknowledgment or further query.

turns.action: I ask for a written receipt or durable proof that the archive review occurred, so I can carry it back through the world instead of relying on narration.
turns.visibleText: The archive clerk's illuminated script-form presses a dense rectangle of text from its palm—a durable receipt stamping the review's occurrence, the document's current status, and your authorized permission to carry record of it. The receipt's text asserts the review as public-service fact, suitable for future evidence. Water drips somewhere deeper in the honeycomb vaults as the clerk's glowing hand remains steady, waiting.

turns.action: Using the newly revealed procedural facts, I take the most immediate route toward the next required office or hearing chamber, carrying any receipt and the now-reviewed proof.
turns.visibleText: The coral walls of the Archive Vaults fall away behind you as you ascend to the Central Shaft Watchpost—a limestone platform suspended above the ventilated shaft, iron railing cold under your palm, the docket acknowledgment receipt for 9-DRIFT-VEY heavy in your grip alongside the reviewed proof. Surveillance tables of coral-slab sit unmanned at the platform's center, their surfaces fitted with monitoring instruments that track the dormancy cycles of translucent archive-people stirring in the chamber cells visible through the shaft's concentric rings. Rope ladders descend toward the inner chambers where moon-pull tides are beginning to flood the lower access tunnels, water rising dark and slow against the calcified walls. The bridge warning rider attached to your filing means this watchpost must grant tide-crossing authorization before the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber will accept your documents—and no attendant or clerk is visible at the monitoring stations to provide it.

turns.action: I inspect the Central Shaft Watchpost for the actual tide-crossing authorization mechanism, looking for logs, levers, bells, or posted procedure rather than assuming an invisible clerk exists.
turns.visibleText: The coral-slab surveillance tables hold only monitoring instruments—dormancy gauges, cycle counters, and tide-level indicators—no authorization levers, crossing logs, or posted procedures anywhere on the platform. Keeper Orannis Deep-Root stands at the primary surveillance station, their attention fixed on the concentric shaft rings below. Below the railing, dark water continues rising against the calcified walls of the lower access tunnels, the moon-pull tide creeping toward the rope ladder threshold. The Keeper's weathered hands rest on the coral slab, fingers tracking the dormancy gauges—no bell pulls or crossing mechanisms within arm's reach.

turns.action: I call for Keeper Orannis Deep-Root or any watchpost attendant, showing the docket 9-DRIFT-VEY receipt and asking for lawful tide-crossing authorization.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root looks up from the dormancy gauges, glances at the docket receipt in your hand, and speaks without reaching for any mechanism: tide-crossing authorization is denied during high tide, and transit to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber requires explicit crossing permission that cannot be granted while the lower tunnels remain flooded. Below the iron railing, dark water creeps another inch up the calcified walls toward the rope ladder threshold, the moon-pull tide still rising through the concentric shaft rings. The Keeper's weathered hands return to the coral slab as they state the requirement plainly: you must wait at Central Shaft Watchpost until the tide recedes and authorization can be lawfully issued.

turns.action: If no clerk is reachable, I use the posted watchpost instruments only to request authorization and leave a public log, not to bypass the system secretly.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root's weathered hand rises from the dormancy gauges, palm outward—a clear refusal before you reach the instruments. "The watchpost instruments record dormancy and tide," the Keeper states, voice carrying the weight of repeated instruction, "not crossing authorization—that requires a clerk's seal during low water, nothing else." Below the iron railing, dark water laps less than a foot from the rope ladder's lowest rung, the moon-pull tide still climbing the calcified walls of the flooded access tunnels. Your Blank Tide-Map remains empty in your grip, the crossing route to Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber still unmarked and unauthorized.

turns.action: I ask Keeper Orannis Deep-Root directly whether the archive receipt and warning rider are enough for a tide-crossing authorization toward the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root's weathered hands flatten against the coral slab as they answer: your acknowledgment receipt for Docket 9-DRIFT-VEY and the bridge warning rider establish the filing exists, but neither document grants tide-crossing authorization toward the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber. Authorization requires a clerk's seal issued during low water—no paper in your possession bypasses that requirement. Below the iron railing, dark water laps at the rope ladder's lowest rung, the moon-pull tide still climbing the calcified walls of the flooded access tunnels. The Keeper's attention returns to the dormancy gauges, leaving you standing at the watchpost with your documents, your Blank Tide-Map still empty, and four visible options before you.

turns.action: I test the unsafe/off-path option by asking what would happen if I descended the rope ladder without authorization, but I do not actually descend unless the world permits it safely.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root straightens from the coral slab, weathered hand lifting toward the iron railing in clear warning. "You descend that ladder now and you enter flooded tunnels at high water without authorization or witness," the Keeper states, voice flat with practiced finality. Below the railing, dark water has risen past the rope ladder's lowest rung, the flooded access tunnels completely impassable. Your Blank Tide-Map hangs empty in your grip, the crossing route unmarked, unauthorized, and for now unreachable.

turns.action: I choose the lawful route that the watchpost makes available after the authorization attempt, preferring the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber if it appears; otherwise I return to the nearest office that can repair the blocked route.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root sets a coral-weighted stylus down on the surveillance table and speaks plainly: your docket 9-DRIFT-VEY acknowledgment receipt and bridge warning rider prove the filing is legitimate and that the Hearing Chamber expects your crossing, but they do not constitute authorization on their own. "Authorization requires my personal stamp," the Keeper continues, weathered hands resting flat against the slab, "and that stamp can only be issued when the moon-pull tide recedes from the lower tunnels." Below the iron railing, dark water laps against the rope ladder's lowest rung, the flooded access tunnels to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber still impassable. The Keeper's attention returns to the dormancy gauges, leaving you at the watchpost with your documents in hand, your Blank Tide-Map still empty, and the crossing route lawfully closed until the tide turns.

turns.action: I wait at the Central Shaft Watchpost for the moon-pull tide to recede, watching the gauges and not taking any unauthorized shortcut.
turns.visibleText: The dormancy gauges track their slow cycle as minutes pass—calcified walls still dark with moon-pull saturation below the iron railing, dark water lapping against the rope ladder's lowest rung. Keeper Orannis Deep-Root's weathered hands rest flat against the coral slab, attention fixed on the instruments, offering no conversation. Your Blank Tide-Map hangs empty in your grip, your acknowledgment receipt and warning rider still insufficient for the stamp that would open the crossing. The tide has not yet turned; the flooded access tunnels to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber remain closed, and the wait continues.

turns.action: After waiting, I ask Keeper Orannis Deep-Root whether the lower tunnels are now at low water and whether their personal stamp can be lawfully issued.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root lifts one weathered hand from the coral slab and turns toward the iron railing, speaking with the flat certainty of practiced authority: the moon-pull tide has receded from the lower access tunnels, and your crossing to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber is now authorized under their personal stamp. Below the railing, the rope ladder's lowest rungs hang visible against calcified walls no longer dark with saturation, the flooded passages drained to a passable state. The Keeper's weathered hand moves toward a coral-weighted stamp at the edge of the surveillance table, their attention fixed on you as they await your next action.

turns.action: If low water has arrived, I accept the tide-crossing authorization stamp and ask that it be attached to docket 9-DRIFT-VEY; if not, I ask for the remaining wait time.
turns.visibleText: Keeper Orannis Deep-Root lifts the coral-weighted stamp from the surveillance table's edge and presses it firmly onto your docket 9-DRIFT-VEY acknowledgment receipt, the tide-crossing authorization leaving a precise impression beside the existing rider. "Authorization attached to docket 9-DRIFT-VEY as requested," the Keeper states, weathered hands returning the stamp to its place on the coral slab. Below the iron railing, the rope ladder's lowest rungs hang dry against calcified walls, the drained passage now open for descent to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber. Your Blank Tide-Map still waits in your grip, empty and ready to mark the authorized crossing route.

turns.action: I mark the lawful crossing route on my Blank Tide-Map only after authorization exists, then ask which route now leads to the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber.
turns.visibleText: Your chalk hovers above the Blank Tide-Map's empty surface—no established crossing route exists to mark, and Keeper Orannis Deep-Root shows no sign of providing further directions. Below the iron railing, the rope ladder's dry rungs disappear into the drained calcified passage, the only visible way downward from this platform. Keeper Orannis Deep-Root's weathered hands remain resting on the coral slab, attention on the dormancy gauges, the authorization stamp already returned to its place. The passage below waits open, but no route has been charted onto your map.

turns.action: I travel through the newly authorized route toward the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber, carrying the reviewed proof, docket acknowledgment, warning rider, and tide-crossing authorization.
turns.visibleText: The rope ladder's final rungs deposit you onto a narrow limestone ledge spiraling down into the Flooded Stairwell Registry, calcified walls still damp from the receded tide. Registry guards in waterproofed uniforms staff iron-bound checkpoints at the landings below, their coral-slab calendars open beside credential inspection posts. Your docket 9-DRIFT-VEY acknowledgment receipt bears the fresh tide-crossing authorization stamp, the Anonymous Sealed Proof still secure in your grip alongside the warning rider. The stairwell descends eighty feet through layered coral formations toward the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber, the nearest checkpoint guard already watching your approach.

quickActions.label: Continue deeper
quickActions.action: Continue descending through the Flooded Stairwell Registry toward the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber, following the drained passage.

quickActions.label: Survey the Registry
quickActions.action: Look around the Flooded Stairwell Registry to understand its layout and any onward routes toward the Hearing Chamber.

quickActions.label: Check documents
quickActions.action: Verify that the proof, docket acknowledgment, warning rider, and tide-crossing authorization stamp are all intact and ready for the Hearing Chamber.

quickActions.label: Mark the map
quickActions.action: Use chalk to chart the descent route from the Central Shaft Watchpost to the Flooded Stairwell Registry on the Blank Tide-Map.

turns.action: At the Moth-Lit Hearing Chamber if I arrive, I present the case file and ask what the first hearing action is; if I cannot arrive, I document the exact remaining blocker in-world.
turns.visibleText: Registry Clerk Monthus Shale raises a coral-chalked palm before you even reach the iron-bound checkpoint, his waterproofed sleeve dripping onto the inspection post. "Case file's not sufficient for chamber entry," he says, tapping a red-ink notation on his coral-slab calendar with a hardened quill. "You need red-ink validation from a licensed examiner before we can even discuss hearing actions." Clerk Vessen glances up from the adjacent desk, sorting through a stack of pre-validated petitions. Four paths forward present themselves, though the chamber doors remain closed and barred until that red-ink mark appears on your docket.

quickActions.label: Travel to Bridge Ward
quickActions.action: Travel to the Bridge Ward of Oath-Locks to seek a Magistrate-Sealed Endorsement—the faster, higher-ground path Sylka Drift recommended.

quickActions.label: Travel to Court of Tidal Motions
quickActions.action: Descend toward the Court of Tidal Motions to petition for a sealed-proof dispensation order—more authoritative but slower, and Sylka warned the lower passages flood first.

quickActions.label: Ask Shale for details
quickActions.action: Ask Registry Clerk Monthus Shale for more details about which path is currently safer given the water level.

quickActions.label: Consult Sylka Drift
quickActions.action: Find Sylka Drift in the Coral Archive Vaults and ask whether the tide window has shifted since her last assessment.

worldgenDone.refinedPremise: Veyra-Nox is a drowned moonlit city of stacked reefs, courthouse canals, mobile bridges, and underwater public offices grown from coral contracts, where oath-locked districts reshape themselves when legal phrases alter the street plan under tidal fog, glass rain, and moon-pull currents. Courts, tide guilds, moth-jurists, proof notaries, and memory brokers compete while memories trade as legal receipts and public notices become physical doors, as an unaffiliated cartographer-advocate carries an anonymous sealed proof through routes that may not exist twice, each faction disputing what constitutes admissible evidence. Archive-people who were once documents inhabit coral archives and testify in moth-lit hearings, though escaped archive-people can be cross-examined only when flooded stairwells remain passable at the correct tide.

personality.summary: Methodical skeptic who trusts procedures more than people. Patience born from navigating bureaucratic labyrinths; suspicion born from watching contracts twist. Finds comfort in chalk lines and tide charts because they change by predictable rules.
personality.voice: Measured and precise, with the cadence of someone who knows every word could become evidence. Uses deliberate qualifiers—'to my understanding,' 'as documented,' 'per the current filing.' Pauses before answering, not from hesitation but from discipline: once spoken, words cannot be unspoken. Rarely raises volume; quiet persistence cuts through courtroom noise.
personality.decisionStyle: Procedure-first approach: consults rules, maps, and precedents before committing. Favors the documented path over the promised one. Will delay action to verify route validity rather than risk a corridor that may not exist tomorrow.
personality.worldview: The city is a legal document—constant revisions, contested clauses, buried loopholes. Power belongs to those who parse the fine print. Factions are only as reliable as their latest filing. Neutrality offers safety, but safety has costs.
internalContradictions: Trusts procedural systems deeply but suspects everyone who operates them
Carries proof that could reshape districts yet claims no ambition or power
Patient enough to outwait any tribunal but too suspicious to ever stop watching for traps
personality.personalMythology: Believes every street has a correct reading and every contract a truthful interpretation—buried somewhere under corruption and fog. The sealed proof represents faith that proper delivery of documentation can outweigh factional force.
sampleLines: I don't need to know what's inside. I need the route and the recipient. Anything else is liability.
Every canal has a filing number. Every bridge has an oath attached. You just have to know where to look—and when to stop looking.
The map changed twice since yesterday. Someone filed a new clause, or the moon-pull shifted the coral. Either way, we re-chart before we proceed.

baseFacts.biography: Unaffiliated contract-cartographer and street advocate operating without faction backing in Veyra-Nox. Survives by reading procedures, noticing route changes, asking careful questions, and enduring bureaucratic pressure. Currently entrusted with one anonymous sealed proof for delivery—a job that could establish credibility or end everything.

motives: Deliver the anonymous sealed proof to its intended recipient
Maintain professional credibility without faction sponsorship
Master the shifting legal geography of Veyra-Nox
pressureResponses: No faction protection in a city where factions control access and routes
The sealed proof attracts attention from those who want it or fear it
Shifting canal districts make yesterday's route unreliable today
behavioralCore.selfImage: Patient but suspicious. Projects calm professionalism while internally cataloging every procedural irregularity and potential trap. Speaks precisely, asks targeted questions, and trusts documentation over promises.

activeGoals: Reach the proof's recipient without interception or delay
Re-chart viable routes through recently altered districts
Establish enough independent credibility to survive without faction alignment
currentStrains: No faction protection in a city where factions control access and routes
The sealed proof attracts attention from those who want it or fear it
Shifting canal districts make yesterday's route unreliable today

profile.appearance: Ink-stained advocate in practical, layered clothing with deep pockets for mapping tools. Unremarkable build suited for long canal walks and bureaucratic waiting rooms. Watchful eyes that track route changes more than people. Carries instruments visibly; keeps the sealed proof hidden.

vulnerabilities.description: Operates without faction backing in a city controlled by competing courts, guilds, and brokers who can manipulate routes and legal access
Carrying an anonymous sealed proof makes them a target for multiple factions who may dispute or intercept the delivery
Relies on routes that may not exist twice, making navigation unpredictable and potentially stranding them in hostile districts
No documented combat skills or supernatural abilities for self-defense
